summary:
The Chechen Wars were two conflicts fought between Russia and the separatist Republic of Ichkeria (Chechnya) in the North Caucasus. The [[event:3484|First Chechen War]] (1994–1996) began when Russian forces invaded to prevent Chechnya's secession following its unilateral declaration of independence after the [[event:2682|dissolution of the Soviet Union]]. Russian forces suffered unexpectedly heavy casualties in a disastrous assault on Grozny in December 1994–January 1995, where poorly coordinated armored columns were destroyed by Chechen fighters in urban combat. A Russian offensive eventually captured most of Chechnya, but Chechen forces recaptured Grozny in August 1996. The Khasavyurt Accord ended the first war, effectively giving Chechnya de facto independence pending a final status determination.

The second war began in August 1999 after Chechen militants invaded neighboring Dagestan and a series of apartment bombings in Russian cities (attributed to Chechen terrorists, though the attribution was contested) killed nearly 300 people. Vladimir Putin, then prime minister, ordered a new invasion and used the military campaign to build his political reputation. Russian forces systematically destroyed Grozny in a prolonged siege and eventually established control over the republic, installing a loyal Chechen administration. The second war's conventional phase was largely complete by 2000, but an insurgency continued for years.

The Chechen wars killed tens of thousands of civilians and combatants and demonstrated both the ruthlessness of Russian counter-insurgency methods and the resilience of Chechen resistance. The conflicts shaped Putin's political rise and presaged the assertive Russian foreign policy that became characteristic of his presidency.
